exercises.title: Why are these secrets storage methods risky?
exercises.instructions:
Rate each method (1=safest, 5=most dangerous):
A: Vault dynamic secrets (1-hour TTL)
B: Kubernetes Secrets (base64)
C: Environment variables in Deployment YAML
D: application.yml in git repository
E: Hardcoded in source code
exercises.expected_solution:
SAFEST to MOST DANGEROUS:
1. A: Vault dynamic secrets — unique per instance, expires 1h, audited, auto-rotated
2. B: Kubernetes Secrets — base64 (not encrypted!), but not in git, RBAC-controlled
   Better with: etcd encryption at rest + External Secrets Operator
3. C: Env vars in Deployment YAML — not in code, but YAML may be in git
   Safer: reference k8s Secret in YAML (not literal value)
4. D: application.yml in git — searchable in git history even after deletion
   Anyone with repo access sees the secret immediately
5. E: Hardcoded in source — WORST: in every clone, every binary, every log
   Impossible to rotate without code change + deploy

test_cases.expected:
SAFEST to MOST DANGEROUS:
1. A: Vault dynamic secrets — unique per instance, expires 1h, audited, auto-rotated
2. B: Kubernetes Secrets — base64 (not encrypted!), but not in git, RBAC-controlled
   Better with: etcd encryption at rest + External Secrets Operator
3. C: Env vars in Deployment YAML — not in code, but YAML may be in git
   Safer: reference k8s Secret in YAML (not literal value)
4. D: application.yml in git — searchable in git history even after deletion
   Anyone with repo access sees the secret immediately
5. E: Hardcoded in source — WORST: in every clone, every binary, every log
   Impossible to rotate without code change + deploy

hints: Dynamic secrets: short TTL limits blast radius
Git history: secrets never truly deleted once committed
k8s Secret: base64 not encrypted — still better than git

exercises.title: Complete the bootstrap.yml Vault config
exercises.instructions: Fill in the Vault k8s authentication config.
exercises.starter_code:
spring.cloud.vault:
  uri: https://vault.razorpay.internal:8200
  authentication: ___
  kubernetes:
    role: ___
  kv:
    enabled: true
    application-name: ___
exercises.expected_solution:
spring.cloud.vault:
  uri: https://vault.razorpay.internal:8200
  authentication: KUBERNETES
  kubernetes:
    role: payment-service
  kv:
    enabled: true
    application-name: payment-service

blanks.hint: auth method using pod's ServiceAccount token
Vault role bound to this service's SA

hints: KUBERNETES auth: most secure for k8s — no static token needed
role: Vault role defines which secrets the service can access

exercises.title: Read secrets from Vault with @Value
exercises.instructions:
Show how Spring Boot reads Vault KV secrets:
1. @Value reads Vault path secret/payment-service
2. dynamic DB credentials via spring.datasource.*
3. What happens at pod restart?
4. @SpringBootTest: verify @Value injection works with test properties
hints: @Value with default: ${prop:default} for test safety
Spring Cloud Vault: maps Vault paths → Spring properties automatically
Dynamic creds: new username generated per pod startup

exercises.title: VaultTemplate: programmatic secret access
exercises.instructions:
Write VaultConfigLoader using VaultTemplate:
1. Read a secret from KV v2 at 'secret/payment-service'
2. Write a secret to Vault (for secret rotation)
3. Check if a path exists
4. Explain: when to use VaultTemplate vs @Value
hints: KV v2: path prefix is 'secret/data/' (note: different from KV v1)
VaultResponse.getData().get('data'): KV v2 nests actual data under 'data' key
@Value: startup injection. VaultTemplate: runtime access

exercises.title: Secret rotation without downtime
exercises.instructions:
Design zero-downtime secret rotation strategy:
1. Old DB password expiring
2. New password generated in Vault
3. Rolling restart: some pods use old, some new password
4. How Vault dynamic secrets handle this automatically
5. @SpringBootTest: demonstrate reload pattern
hints: Dynamic Vault: rotation = restart pod → new credential automatically
Multiple valid DB users: old pod's user + new pod's user both valid during rolling restart
Vault PKI engine: also handles TLS certificate rotation

exercises.title: Vault AppRole authentication for local development
exercises.instructions:
Configure Vault AppRole auth for local dev (no k8s SA available):
1. bootstrap.yml: AppRole auth with role_id + secret_id
2. Secret IDs from environment variables
3. Explain: why AppRole not used in production
4. @SpringBootTest: verify auth method config
exercises.expected_solution:
# bootstrap.yml for LOCAL DEV (AppRole):
spring:
  cloud:
    vault:
      uri: http://localhost:8200  # local dev Vault
      authentication: APPROLE
      app-role:
        role-id: ${VAULT_ROLE_ID}       # from 'vault read auth/approle/role/dev/role-id'
        secret-id: ${VAULT_SECRET_ID}   # from 'vault write -f auth/approle/role/dev/secret-id'
        # Store these in .env file (NEVER commit!)

# WHY KUBERNETES AUTH IN PRODUCTION (not AppRole):
# AppRole: role_id + secret_id must be stored somewhere
#   → secret_id is itself a secret that needs secure storage (circular problem!)
#   → if secret_id leaks: attacker can authenticate as the service
# Kubernetes auth: uses k8s SA token (automatically issued by k8s, no static value)
#   → no secret to store! k8s SA token is mounted automatically into pods
#   → even if pod is compromised: SA token only valid within k8s cluster
#   → rotation: k8s rotates SA tokens automatically

# LOCAL DEV WORKFLOW:
# 1. Start local Vault: docker run -p 8200:8200 vault:latest
# 2. vault auth enable approle
# 3. vault write auth/approle/role/dev policies=dev-policy
# 4. vault read auth/approle/role/dev/role-id  → VAULT_ROLE_ID
# 5. vault write -f auth/approle/role/dev/secret-id → VAULT_SECRET_ID
# 6. Add to .env: VAULT_ROLE_ID=xxx VAULT_SECRET_ID=yyy

public class AppRoleGuide { public static void main(String[] a){
    System.out.println("AppRole: local dev. Kubernetes auth: production.");
} }

expected_stdout_contains: AppRole: local dev. Kubernetes auth: production.

hints: AppRole needs role_id + secret_id — both must be stored somewhere (circular)
Kubernetes auth: no static credential needed (SA token auto-mounted)
Local dev: AppRole fine since k8s SA not available

exercises.title: Vault policy for payment service
exercises.instructions:
Write Vault policy for payment-service:
1. Read-only: secret/data/payment-service (API keys)
2. Read: database/creds/payment-service-db (dynamic DB creds)
3. Deny: everything else (least privilege)
4. Explain: why least privilege matters
5. Show: how to apply the policy

expected_stdout_contains: Vault policy: least privilege per service

hints: capabilities=[read]: read-only access — compromised service cannot modify secrets
Implicit deny: anything not listed is denied
Separate policy per service: payment-service cannot read fraud-service secrets

exercises.title: External Secrets Operator: Vault → k8s Secret
exercises.instructions:
Design External Secrets Operator setup:
1. ClusterSecretStore: connects to Vault
2. ExternalSecret: syncs Vault path to k8s Secret
3. Refresh interval: 1 hour
4. Spring Boot: reads k8s Secret as env var
5. Explain: why ESO vs Spring Cloud Vault direct
exercises.expected_solution:
# ClusterSecretStore: Vault backend
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ClusterSecretStore
metadata:
  name: vault-backend
spec:
  provider:
    vault:
      server: 'https://vault.razorpay.internal:8200'
      path: 'secret'
      version: 'v2'
      auth:
        kubernetes:
          mountPath: 'kubernetes'
          role: 'eso-reader'  # special ESO service account role
---
# ExternalSecret: sync payment-service secrets to k8s Secret
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: payment-secrets
  namespace: payments
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h
  secretStoreRef: { name: vault-backend, kind: ClusterSecretStore }
  target:
    name: payment-vault-secrets  # creates/updates this k8s Secret
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
  - secretKey: visa_api_key
    remoteRef: { key: payment-service, property: visa_api_key }
  - secretKey: redis_password
    remoteRef: { key: payment-service, property: redis_password }

# Spring Boot reads the k8s Secret as env var:
# VISA_API_KEY → spring.payment.visa.api-key
# No Spring Cloud Vault dependency needed!

# ESO vs Spring Cloud Vault direct:
# Spring Cloud Vault: each service pod calls Vault directly
#   Pros: simpler setup, dynamic secrets
#   Cons: every pod is a Vault client (auth overhead)
# ESO: one ESO controller fetches from Vault, creates k8s Secrets
#   Pros: apps don't need Vault client code, works with any language
#   Cons: secrets stored in k8s (base64), not dynamic
# Use ESO for: non-Java services, orgs standardizing on k8s Secrets
# Use Spring Cloud Vault for: dynamic DB secrets, Java-specific advanced features

public class ESOvsVaultDirect { public static void main(String[] a){
    System.out.println("ESO: language-agnostic. Spring Cloud Vault: Java-native dynamic secrets.");
} }

expected_stdout_contains: ESO: language-agnostic. Spring Cloud Vault: Java-native dynamic secrets.

hints: ESO: Vault → k8s Secret (one central controller)
Spring Cloud Vault: each app directly to Vault (dynamic, more features)
refreshInterval: 1h: ESO refreshes the k8s Secret from Vault every hour

exercises.title: Secret auditing and access logging
exercises.instructions:
Design a SecretAuditService:
1. VaultTemplate: list all active leases for payment-service
2. Log: lease ID, expiry, path for each secret access
3. Alert: if any lease is within 5 minutes of expiry (not auto-renewed)
4. @SpringBootTest: audit service instantiates
hints: Vault audit log: records all access automatically (enable audit backend)
Application-level audit: supplement (not replace) Vault's built-in audit
NEVER log secret values — only paths, lease IDs, and timestamps

exercises.title: What happens in these Vault failure scenarios?
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, what happens to the payment service?
1. Vault goes down during normal operation (service already started)
2. Vault goes down at service startup
3. Dynamic DB credential expires but Vault can't renew (network issue)
4. Service's Vault policy is revoked
5. Vault comes back after 2 hours of downtime
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Vault DOWN during normal operation:
   Spring Boot already started with injected secrets → @Value beans unaffected.
   HikariCP: using already-obtained dynamic credentials → continues working.
   At TTL renewal time (80% of 1h = 48min): Spring Cloud Vault tries to renew → fails.
   After TTL expires: HikariCP connections fail (DB rejects expired credentials).
   Mitigation: set longer TTL (4h) or implement fallback to static emergency credentials.

2. Vault DOWN at startup:
   Spring Boot cannot fetch secrets → BeanCreationException on @Value injection.
   Service FAILS TO START. Pods enter CrashLoopBackOff.
   Mitigation: Spring Cloud Vault retry config: vault.retry.max-attempts=5
   Or: cache secrets in k8s InitContainer with ESO → startup doesn't need live Vault.

3. Dynamic DB credential can't renew:
   At 80% TTL: renewal attempt fails (network issue).
   Spring Cloud Vault retries with backoff.
   At 100% TTL: credential expires → DB connections start failing.
   HikariCP: getConnection() throws → requests fail with 503.
   If Vault recovers within TTL: renewal succeeds, normal operation resumes.

4. Service's Vault policy revoked:
   Next renewal attempt → 403 Forbidden from Vault.
   Spring Cloud Vault: logs error, cannot renew.
   Credentials expire → service can't get new ones → DB failure.
   This is the INTENDED behavior for incident response: revoke all credentials.

5. Vault recovers after 2 hours:
   Dynamic credentials expired during outage (1h TTL).
   Spring Cloud Vault re-authenticates to Vault (k8s SA token still valid).
   Requests new dynamic credentials → HikariCP reconnects with new credentials.
   Service recovers automatically (no manual intervention needed).

test_cases.expected:
1. Vault DOWN during normal operation:
   Spring Boot already started with injected secrets → @Value beans unaffected.
   HikariCP: using already-obtained dynamic credentials → continues working.
   At TTL renewal time (80% of 1h = 48min): Spring Cloud Vault tries to renew → fails.
   After TTL expires: HikariCP connections fail (DB rejects expired credentials).
   Mitigation: set longer TTL (4h) or implement fallback to static emergency credentials.

2. Vault DOWN at startup:
   Spring Boot cannot fetch secrets → BeanCreationException on @Value injection.
   Service FAILS TO START. Pods enter CrashLoopBackOff.
   Mitigation: Spring Cloud Vault retry config: vault.retry.max-attempts=5
   Or: cache secrets in k8s InitContainer with ESO → startup doesn't need live Vault.

3. Dynamic DB credential can't renew:
   At 80% TTL: renewal attempt fails (network issue).
   Spring Cloud Vault retries with backoff.
   At 100% TTL: credential expires → DB connections start failing.
   HikariCP: getConnection() throws → requests fail with 503.
   If Vault recovers within TTL: renewal succeeds, normal operation resumes.

4. Service's Vault policy revoked:
   Next renewal attempt → 403 Forbidden from Vault.
   Spring Cloud Vault: logs error, cannot renew.
   Credentials expire → service can't get new ones → DB failure.
   This is the INTENDED behavior for incident response: revoke all credentials.

5. Vault recovers after 2 hours:
   Dynamic credentials expired during outage (1h TTL).
   Spring Cloud Vault re-authenticates to Vault (k8s SA token still valid).
   Requests new dynamic credentials → HikariCP reconnects with new credentials.
   Service recovers automatically (no manual intervention needed).

hints: Vault at startup failure: service won't start — add retry + fallback config
TTL expiry during outage: service degrades; recovers when Vault comes back
Policy revoke: intentional for incident response — stops the compromised service

exercises.title: Complete Vault integration: @Value + VaultTemplate + audit
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Vault integration:
1. VaultSecretService: @Value for API keys, VaultTemplate for dynamic path
2. SecretAuditFilter: log every secret access (path+time, NOT value)
3. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): @Value injected, audit log doesn't contain secret value
4. All 2 tests pass
hints: Audit log assertion: verify secret value NOT in log (security test)
@Value with default: test-safe without real Vault
VaultTemplate: try-catch for test environment (no Vault running)

exercises.title: Vault PKI: dynamic TLS certificates
exercises.instructions:
Document Vault PKI engine for TLS certificate management:
1. Configure Vault PKI for internal service-to-service TLS
2. Spring Boot: load certificate from Vault PKI
3. Certificate auto-renewal before expiry
4. Explain: mTLS (mutual TLS) using Vault PKI

expected_stdout_contains: Vault PKI: auto-managed TLS certs with 24h TTL

hints: PKI engine: issues TLS certs from Vault CA
mTLS: both sides present certs — cert = identity (no JWT needed for service auth)
Spring Cloud Vault PKI: auto-renewal before expiry (hot-reload SSL context)

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay secrets architecture
exercises.instructions:
Design complete Razorpay secrets architecture:
1. @Value from Vault KV (API keys)
2. Dynamic DB credentials (Vault database engine)
3. SecretRotationService: trigger rotation on demand
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): @Value, rotation service, audit log
5. All 3 pass
hints: maskedVisa: return first 8 chars + *** (show type but not full value)
rotationLog: in-memory for test (production calls Vault rotate-root API)
audit hasNoSecretValues: security test — verify values never in log

exercises.title: Vault HA (High Availability) resilience
exercises.instructions:
Design resilience for Vault HA setup:
1. Spring Cloud Vault retry config (max 5 attempts, backoff)
2. Emergency fallback: read from k8s Secret if Vault unavailable
3. @Scheduled: health check Vault every 30s
4. @SpringBootTest: fallback activates when VaultTemplate throws
hints: fail-fast: false: allow startup without Vault (use fallback)
Fallback from env var: k8s Secret provides emergency credentials
vaultHealthy flag: circuit breaker pattern for Vault calls

exercises.title: Complete M23 T3 Vault synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay Vault synthesis:
1. @Value injection (API key with default for tests)
2. SecretAuditService: log access without logging values
3. VaultResilienceService: fallback when Vault unavailable
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): value injected, audit no-value, fallback works
5. All 3 pass
hints: rzp_ prefix → 'live' type: simple string check works in test
valuesNeverLogged: security assertion — audit log must not contain secret
VaultTemplate in SpringBootTest: fails (no real Vault) → fallback activates
